title: Extractor-based Approach to Proving Memory-Sample Lower Bounds for Learning
speaker: Sumegha Garg
abstract: A recent line of work has focused on the following question: Can one prove strong unconditional lower bounds on the number of samples needed for learning under memory constraints? We study an extractor-based approach to proving such bounds for a large class of learning problems as follows.

proof_stages.label: Streaming learning under memory constraints
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames the problem of proving unconditional sample lower bounds for bounded-memory streaming learners, using parity learning and previous memory-sample tradeoff results as the benchmark setting.

proof_stages.label: Matrix extractor formulation of learning
proof_stages.summary: A learning problem is encoded as a sign matrix with rows as samples and columns as concepts, and a low-bias large-submatrix extractor property is stated as implying that every learner needs either Ω(k l) memory or 2^{Ω(r)} samples.

proof_stages.label: Polynomial-learning consequences
proof_stages.summary: The extractor theorem is applied to learning low-degree polynomials over F_2 and to the transposed problem of learning from low-degree equations, yielding larger polynomial memory lower bounds paired with exponential sample lower bounds.

proof_stages.label: Branching-program reduction and significant beliefs
proof_stages.summary: A bounded-memory learner is modeled as a layered branching program, with each vertex carrying a posterior distribution on the hidden concept, and high-L2 posterior vertices are identified as significant states whose reachability controls success.

proof_stages.label: Truncated paths and bad edges
proof_stages.summary: The proof replaces true computation paths by truncated paths that stop on atypical events, especially samples whose label is too predictable from the current belief and would therefore reveal too much information.

proof_stages.label: Progress-function bound for one-pass learners
proof_stages.summary: For each significant vertex a progress function measures closeness to reaching it, and the extractor property shows this quantity grows slowly enough that any fixed significant vertex is reached with probability exponentially small in k l, allowing a union bound over low-memory programs.

proof_stages.label: Two-pass reduction via product branching programs
proof_stages.summary: In the two-pass setting, reused samples destroy the uniformity assumptions behind bad-edge and progress arguments, so the proof uses a read-once product of the first- and second-pass branching programs together with new high-probability-edge stopping rules and a one-pass subroutine to obtain the weaker two-pass lower bound.

proof_stages.label: Limits and open directions beyond the extractor method
proof_stages.summary: The closing discussion asks for methods beyond the present extractor framework, including tighter noisy-parity bounds, possible communication-complexity perspectives, and extensions to more passes or read-twice branching programs.

examples.name: Parity learning toy model
examples.note: Random linear equations over F_2 make the streaming learning model, the memory/sample tradeoff, and the extractor-matrix viewpoint concrete; the talk does not derive the extractor method from this example alone.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker defines the parity learning problem, tries a small five-bit instance with equations, analyzes concrete learner strategies, and later runs the two-pass branching-program framework on parity.

examples.name: Bounded-storage cryptographic scheme from parity lower bounds
examples.note: The parity lower bound is described as yielding unconditional security against bounded-memory attackers, illustrating why memory-sample lower bounds matter beyond learning theory.
examples.engagement_reason: The bounded-storage cryptographic scheme is cited as an application of the parity lower bound with stated parameters, but no construction or proof is worked through.

passes.reason: The bounded-storage scheme is a standard application of the parity lower bound, not a sporadic, limiting, or adversarial object.

examples.name: Learning low-degree polynomials over F_2

examples.engagement_reason: Learning low-degree polynomials over F_2 is described and its lower bound is stated, but the extractor property or parameters are not verified on this example.

examples.name: Learning from low-degree equations
examples.note: The transpose of the polynomial-learning matrix gives a second application where the learner recovers an n-bit vector from random low-degree polynomial equations.

examples.name: Noisy random linear equations
examples.note: The noisy parity problem is offered as a benchmark open problem whose tight lower bound would require techniques beyond the current extractor framework.
examples.engagement_reason: Noisy random linear equations appear only as an open problem with desired and current bounds stated, not analyzed in detail.

examples.name: Number-on-forehead communication-complexity analogy
examples.note: An audience question proposes multiparty communication lower-bound techniques as an analogy, but the discussion emphasizes differences rather than extracting a new proof step.
